questions.text: According to the talk, how did the Green Revolution affect global trade?
choices: It enabled some nations to shift from importing to exporting food crops.
It reduced the overall amount of grain available for international markets.
It created trade conflicts between developing and industrialized countries over fertilizer use.
It encouraged governments to ban the export of genetically modified seeds.

questions.text: Why does the speaker mention India and Mexico?
choices: To highlight places where fertilizers and pesticides most impacted the population’s health
To compare regions that depended on imports for survival
To illustrate countries where innovation increased food production
To show where farming diversity has been replaced with innovative technology

questions.text: According to the talk, what was one major drawback of the Green Revolution?
choices: It failed to improve overall grain yields in developing regions.
It widened economic gaps between large commercial farms and smaller producers.
It encouraged countries to abandon traditional irrigation and crop systems.
It caused environmental damage through heavy use of fertilizers and pesticides.

questions.text: What does the speaker imply about the long-term results of the Green Revolution?
choices: It solved food shortages but created new environmental challenges.
It ended economic inequality among farmers in poorer nations.
It prevented the use of modern irrigation systems that are better for the environment.
It had no significant effect on global trade until the latter half of the 20th century.

A psychological phenomenon related to unfinished tasks

They remembered unpaid orders better than completed ones.
They relied on repetition rather than reasoning to recall customer requests.
They retained details longer when the café was quiet and less crowded.

They often strengthen unrelated cognitive skills.
They tend to occupy the mind until they are completed.
They typically cause the brain to block other incoming information.

questions.text: What will the speaker most likely discuss next?
choices: Modern tools and techniques designed to help people stay focused
The origins of psychological research in early twentieth-century Europe
The relationship between memory and physical health in older adults
The use of experimental methods in unrelated areas of psychology

questions.text: What is the main topic of the talk?

The invention of mechanical clocks in Europe and its impact on culture
How religion shaped early calendars and cultures

questions.text: What can be inferred about the social function of timekeeping systems?
choices: They have little influence on human behavior
They are developed mainly for religious control
They reveal the cultural values and priorities of a society
They remain unchanged despite technological progress

questions.text: Why does the speaker mention the ringing of bells in medieval towns?

To show how they organized people’s daily lives
To describe an early form of communication between towns
To explain why clock towers became symbols of wealth

questions.text: According to the speaker, what change did mechanical clocks bring to society?
choices: They reduced the need for written records of time.
They replaced religious calendars and ceremonies.
They ended the use of natural rhythms for agricultural production.
They helped organize daily routines and emphasized punctuality.

questions.text: What is the main topic of the talk?
choices: How animals survive cold temperatures through hibernation
How water can remain liquid below its normal freezing point
Why most liquids freeze at the same temperature
How understanding the way ice forms leads to scientific breakthroughs

questions.text: What does the speaker say about the wood frog?
choices: It survives winter by preventing ice from forming in vital organs.
It keeps its body temperature above freezing by generating heat.
It moves underground to escape extreme cold.
It breathes through its skin while frozen.

questions.text: What can be inferred about scientists’ interest in studying supercooling?

They are primarily focused on protecting animals from extreme cold.
They are trying to develop new methods to heat frozen environments.
They hope to apply natural freezing strategies to medical storage.

questions.text: Why does the speaker describe the wood frog in detail?

To compare animal hibernation with freezing
To illustrate how supercooling functions in a living organism
To explain why frogs are found in colder climates

questions.text: What is the main topic of the talk?

questions.text: What does the speaker say about desert plants?

They release moisture only at night to conserve water and regulate temperature.

questions.text: What can be inferred about evaporation’s importance in engineering?

It is primarily used to generate heat for large industrial machines.
It provides a natural model for designing efficient cooling technologies.
It is useful in humid environments with high water content.

questions.text: Why does the speaker compare sweating to evaporation in the atmosphere?

To contrast the ways natural and human processes release energy
To argue that evaporation works differently in living organisms
To show that the same physical principle operates at both small and global scales
